title: ACAS Code of Practice 1: Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures
issuedUnder: Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992 s.199
chapters: 47 paragraphs · Issued under TULRCA 1992 s.199 · Laid before Parliament 16 January 2015 · In effect 11 March 2015
desc: The ACAS Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures is the single most important procedural guidance document in UK employment law. It sets out the minimum standards of reasonable behaviour for handling disciplinary and grievance situations in the workplace. While a failure to follow the Code does not in itself create liability, employment tribunals must take it into account when considering relevant cases and may adjust awards by up to 25% for unreasonable failure to comply with any provision. The Code establishes the foundational procedural requirements: establish the facts (investigate), inform the employee in writing, hold a meeting, allow the right to be accompanied, decide and inform, and provide a right of appeal. The Code does not apply to redundancy dismissals or non-renewal of fixed-term contracts. It is issued under section 199 of the Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992.
legalStatus: Statutory Code of Practice. A failure to follow the Code does not, in itself, make a person or organisation liable to proceedings. However, employment tribunals will take the Code into account when considering relevant cases. Tribunals may adjust awards by up to 25% for unreasonable failure to comply with any provision of the Code (TULRCA 1992 s.207A).

sections.title: Purpose, scope and legal status
sections.summary: The Code provides basic practical guidance to employers, employees and their representatives and sets out principles for handling disciplinary and grievance situations in the workplace. Disciplinary situations include misconduct and poor performance. Grievances are concerns, problems or complaints that employees raise with their employers. The Code does not apply to redundancy dismissals or the non-renewal of fixed-term contracts on their expiry. A failure to follow the Code does not in itself create liability, but tribunals will take it into account and may adjust awards by up to 25% for unreasonable failure to comply.
sections.text:
1. This Code is designed to help employers, employees and their representatives deal with disciplinary and grievance situations in the workplace.

2. Disciplinary situations include misconduct and/or poor performance. If employers have a separate capability procedure they may prefer to address performance issues under this procedure. If so, however, the basic principles of fairness set out in this Code should still be followed, albeit that they may need to be adapted.

3. Grievances are concerns, problems or complaints that employees raise with their employers. The Code does not apply to redundancy dismissals or the non-renewal of fixed-term contracts on their expiry.

4. Many potential disciplinary or grievance issues can be resolved informally. A quiet word is often all that is required to resolve an issue. However, where an issue cannot be resolved informally then it may be pursued formally.

5. This Code sets out the basic requirements of fairness that will be applicable in most cases; it is intended to provide the standard of reasonable behaviour in most instances.

versions.description: Previous Code of Practice (2009 edition) came into effect following the repeal of the statutory dispute resolution procedures by the Employment Act 2008.
Current Code came into effect. Replaces the 2009 edition. Issued under TULRCA 1992 s.199. Laid before Parliament 16 January 2015.

leadingCases.name: Polkey v AE Dayton Services Ltd [1988] ICR 142
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords established that procedural fairness is an integral part of the determination of whether a dismissal is fair. An employer cannot argue that a failure to follow fair procedure made no difference to the outcome. However, a tribunal may reduce compensation where the employee would have been dismissed in any event had a fair procedure been followed.
leadingCases.significance: Foundation authority on the importance of fair procedure in unfair dismissal. The 'Polkey reduction' principle derives from this case.

leadingCases.name: Sarkar v West London Mental Health NHS Trust [2010] EWCA Civ 289
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal confirmed that the ACAS Code is the starting point for assessing procedural fairness in unfair dismissal cases. A failure to follow the Code will be a significant factor in assessing whether the employer acted reasonably under s.98(4) ERA 1996.
leadingCases.significance: Confirmed the ACAS Code as the starting point for procedural fairness assessment

refs: TULRCA 1992 s.199 (ACAS codes of practice)
TULRCA 1992 s.207A (effect of failure to comply with Code — 25% adjustment)
ERA 1996 s.98(4) (test of fairness in unfair dismissal)
https://www.acas.org.uk/acas-code-of-practice-on-disciplinary-and-grievance-procedures/html

parts.title: Keys to Handling Disciplinary Issues in the Workplace

sections.title: Establish the facts of each case — investigation
sections.summary: It is important to carry out necessary investigations of potential disciplinary matters without unreasonable delay to establish the facts of the case. In some cases this will require the holding of an investigatory meeting with the employee before proceeding to any disciplinary hearing. In misconduct cases, where practicable, different people should carry out the investigation and disciplinary hearing. If there is an investigatory meeting, this should not by itself result in any disciplinary action. Although there is no statutory right to be accompanied at a formal investigatory meeting, the employer should allow it where the meeting could result in formal disciplinary action or where a reasonable request is made.
sections.text:
5. It is important to carry out necessary investigations of potential disciplinary matters without unreasonable delay to establish the facts of the case.

6. In some cases this will require the holding of an investigatory meeting with the employee before proceeding to any disciplinary hearing. In misconduct cases, where practicable, different people should carry out the investigation and disciplinary hearing.

7. If there is an investigatory meeting this should not by itself result in any disciplinary action. Although there is no statutory right to be accompanied at a formal investigatory meeting, such a filtered right should be allowed where a reasonable request is made.

8. In cases where a period of suspension with pay is considered necessary, this period should be as brief as possible, should be kept under review, and it should be made clear that this suspension is not considered a disciplinary action.

leadingCases.name: Sainsbury's Supermarkets Ltd v Hitt [2003] ICR 111
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the range of reasonable responses test applies not only to the decision to dismiss but also to the investigation. The question is whether the investigation fell within the range of reasonable investigations that a reasonable employer would have carried out, not whether the tribunal would have investigated differently.
leadingCases.significance: Extended the range of reasonable responses test to the investigation stage of the disciplinary process

leadingCases.name: A v B [2003] IRLR 405
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the standard of investigation required depends on the gravity of the charges. More serious allegations require a more thorough investigation. In cases involving allegations of a criminal nature, the investigation should be as searching as the circumstances reasonably allow.
leadingCases.significance: Established proportionality principle — more serious allegations require more thorough investigation

refs: ERA 1996 s.98(4) (reasonableness of investigation as part of fairness test)
BHS v Burchell [1980] ICR 303 (three-stage test: genuine belief, reasonable grounds, reasonable investigation)
https://www.acas.org.uk/acas-code-of-practice-on-disciplinary-and-grievance-procedures/html

sections.title: Inform the employee of the problem — written notification
sections.summary: If it is decided that there is a disciplinary case to answer, the employee should be notified of this in writing. This notification should contain sufficient information about the alleged misconduct or poor performance and its possible consequences to enable the employee to prepare to answer the case at a disciplinary meeting. It would normally be appropriate to provide copies of any written evidence, which may include any witness statements, with the notification. The notification should give details of the time and venue for the disciplinary meeting and advise the employee of their right to be accompanied at the meeting.
sections.text:
9. If it is decided that there is a disciplinary case to answer, the employee should be notified of this in writing. This notification should contain sufficient information about the alleged misconduct or poor performance and its possible consequences to enable the employee to prepare to answer the case at a disciplinary meeting. It would normally be appropriate to provide copies of any written evidence, which may include any witness statements, with the notification.

10. The notification should give details of the time and venue for the disciplinary meeting and advise the employee of their right to be accompanied at the meeting.

11. Where the employer or employee intends to call relevant witnesses they should give advance notice that they intend to do this.

leadingCases.name: Strouthos v London Underground Ltd [2004] IRLR 636
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that an employee must be given sufficient detail of the charges against them to be able to respond properly. The charges on which the employer proceeds should be clearly articulated. If the charges are changed or widened during the process, the employee must be given an opportunity to respond to the new charges.
leadingCases.significance: Key authority on adequacy of notification — employee must know the precise charges to prepare a defence

refs: ECHR Art.6 (right to a fair hearing — natural justice principles apply by analogy)
https://www.acas.org.uk/acas-code-of-practice-on-disciplinary-and-grievance-procedures/html

sections.title: Hold a meeting with the employee — the disciplinary hearing
sections.summary: The meeting should be held without unreasonable delay whilst allowing the employee reasonable time to prepare their case. At the meeting the employer should explain the complaint and go through the evidence. The employee should be allowed to set out their case and answer any allegations, ask questions, present evidence, call witnesses, and raise points about information provided by witnesses. Where possible, the employer should allow the employee to be accompanied by a companion. The meeting may be adjourned to allow further investigation if new facts come to light.
sections.text:
12. The meeting should be held without unreasonable delay whilst allowing the employee reasonable time to prepare their case.

13. Employers and employees (and their companions) should make every effort to attend the meeting. At the meeting the employer should explain the complaint against the employee and go through the evidence that has been gathered. The employee should be allowed to set out their case and answer any allegations that have been made. The employee should also be given a reasonable opportunity to ask questions, present evidence and call witnesses. They should also be given an opportunity to raise points about any information provided by witnesses.

14. Where an employer or employee intends to call relevant witnesses they should give advance notice that they intend to do this.

15. The statutory right to be accompanied by a companion applies specifically to disciplinary hearings which could result in a formal warning being issued, the taking of some other disciplinary action, or the confirmation of a warning or some other disciplinary action (appeal hearings).

16. Workers have a statutory right to be accompanied by a companion where the disciplinary meeting could result in—
a formal warning being issued; or
the taking of some other disciplinary action; or
the confirmation of a warning or some other disciplinary action (appeal hearings).

17. The chosen companion may be a fellow worker, a trade union representative, or an official employed by a trade union. A trade union representative who is not an employed official must have been certified by their union as being competent to accompany a worker.

leadingCases.name: Talon Engineering Ltd v Smith [2018] UKEAT/0236/17
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT considered the employer's duty to accommodate postponement requests when the employee's chosen companion is unavailable. The employer must take reasonable steps to accommodate the companion's availability, but is not required to postpone indefinitely.
leadingCases.significance: Clarified the employer's obligation regarding companion availability and hearing postponement

leadingCases.name: Toal v GB Oils Ltd [2013] UKEAT/0569/12
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the right to be accompanied under ERA 1996 s.10 is limited to a fellow worker or trade union representative. There is no right to be accompanied by a legal representative at a disciplinary hearing under the statutory provisions.
leadingCases.significance: Confirmed that the statutory right to accompaniment does not extend to legal representatives

refs: ERA 1996 s.10 (right to be accompanied at disciplinary and grievance hearings)
ERA 1996 s.11–12 (complaint to tribunal about right to be accompanied)
TULRCA 1992 s.207A (25% adjustment for failure to comply with Code)
https://www.acas.org.uk/acas-code-of-practice-on-disciplinary-and-grievance-procedures/html

sections.title: Decide on appropriate action
sections.summary: After the meeting, decide whether or not disciplinary action is justified and inform the employee accordingly in writing. Where misconduct is confirmed or the employee is found to be performing unsatisfactorily, usual warnings include: a first written warning (or improvement note for performance), a final written warning, and dismissal or other sanction (such as demotion, disciplinary transfer, or loss of seniority). A decision to dismiss should only be taken by a manager who has the authority to do so. The employee should be informed in writing of the reasons for the dismissal, the date on which employment will terminate, and the right of appeal.
sections.text:
18. After the meeting decide whether or not disciplinary or any other action is justified and inform the employee accordingly in writing.

19. Where misconduct is confirmed or the employee is found to be performing unsatisfactorily it is usual to give the employee a written warning. A further act of misconduct or failure to improve performance within a set period would normally result in a final written warning.

20. If an employee's first misconduct or unsatisfactory performance is sufficiently serious, it may be appropriate to move directly to a final written warning. This might occur where the employee's actions have had, or are liable to have, a serious or harmful impact on the organisation.

21. A first or final written warning should set out the nature of the misconduct or poor performance and the change in behaviour or improvement in performance required (with timescale). The employee should be told how long the warning will remain current. The employee should be informed of the consequences of further misconduct, or failure to improve performance, within the set period following a final warning. For instance that it may result in dismissal or some other contractual penalty such as demotion or loss of seniority.

22. Where a warning is issued, the employee should be informed in writing of the reasons for the warning, that it is part of the formal disciplinary process, and that they may appeal against the warning.

23. Some acts, termed gross misconduct, are so serious in themselves or have such serious consequences that they may call for dismissal without notice for a first offence. But a fair disciplinary process should always be followed, before dismissing for gross misconduct.

24. Disciplinary rules should give examples of acts which the employer regards as acts of gross misconduct. These may vary according to the nature of the organisation and what it does, but might include things such as theft or fraud, physical violence, gross negligence, or serious insubordination.

leadingCases.name: British Home Stores Ltd v Burchell [1980] ICR 303
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT established the three-stage test for misconduct dismissals: (1) the employer must have genuinely believed the employee was guilty of the misconduct; (2) the employer must have had reasonable grounds for that belief; and (3) at the time of forming that belief, the employer must have carried out as much investigation into the matter as was reasonable in the circumstances.
leadingCases.significance: The definitive three-stage test for misconduct dismissals — cited in virtually every unfair dismissal case involving misconduct

leadingCases.name: Iceland Frozen Foods Ltd v Jones [1983] ICR 17
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the function of the tribunal is to determine whether the decision to dismiss fell within the range of reasonable responses that a reasonable employer might have adopted. The tribunal must not substitute its own view for that of the employer.
leadingCases.significance: Established the 'range of reasonable responses' test that governs all unfair dismissal assessments

leadingCases.name: Brito-Babapulle v Ealing Hospital NHS Trust [2013] UKEAT/0358/12
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT confirmed that even in cases of gross misconduct, a fair procedure must be followed before dismissal. Summary dismissal without following a fair process will normally be unfair regardless of the seriousness of the conduct.
leadingCases.significance: Confirmed that gross misconduct does not excuse procedural shortcuts

refs: ERA 1996 s.86 (minimum notice periods)
ERA 1996 s.98 (fairness of dismissal)
ERA 1996 s.98(4) (range of reasonable responses test)
https://www.acas.org.uk/acas-code-of-practice-on-disciplinary-and-grievance-procedures/html

sections.title: Provide employees with an opportunity to appeal
sections.summary: Where an employee feels that disciplinary action taken against them is wrong or unjust they should appeal against the decision. Appeals should be heard without unreasonable delay and ideally at an agreed time and place. Employees should let employers know the grounds for their appeal in writing. The appeal should be dealt with impartially and, wherever possible, by a manager who has not previously been involved in the case. Workers have a statutory right to be accompanied at appeal hearings. The employee should be informed in writing of the results of the appeal hearing as soon as possible.
sections.text:
25. Where an employee feels that disciplinary action taken against them is wrong or unjust they should appeal against the decision. Appeals should be heard without unreasonable delay and ideally at an agreed time and place. Employees should let employers know the grounds for their appeal in writing.

26. The appeal should be dealt with impartially and, wherever possible, by a manager who has not previously been involved in the case.

27. Workers have a statutory right to be accompanied at appeal hearings.

28. The employee should be informed in writing of the results of the appeal hearing as soon as possible.

leadingCases.name: Taylor v OCS Group Ltd [2006] EWCA Civ 702
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the fairness of a dismissal must be assessed by looking at the disciplinary process as a whole, including the appeal. Defects in the original hearing may be cured by a fair appeal, and conversely an otherwise fair process may be rendered unfair by a defective appeal.
leadingCases.significance: Established that the appeal is an integral part of the disciplinary process and can cure earlier defects

refs: ERA 1996 s.10 (right to be accompanied at appeal hearings)
West Midlands Co-operative Society v Tipton [1986] ICR 192 (appeal as part of overall fairness assessment)
https://www.acas.org.uk/acas-code-of-practice-on-disciplinary-and-grievance-procedures/html

sections.title: Letting the employer know the nature of the grievance — formal notification
sections.summary: If it is not possible to resolve a grievance informally employees should raise the matter formally and without unreasonable delay with a manager who is not the subject of the grievance. This should be done in writing and should set out the nature of the grievance.
sections.text:
32. If it is not possible to resolve a grievance informally employees should raise the matter formally and without unreasonable delay with a manager who is not the subject of the grievance. This should be done in writing and should set out the nature of the grievance.

33. Employers should arrange for a formal meeting to be held without unreasonable delay after a grievance is received.

34. Employers, employees and their companions should make every effort to attend the meeting. Employees should be allowed to explain their grievance and how they think it should be resolved. Consideration should be given to adjourning the meeting for any investigation that may be necessary.
refs: ERA 1996 s.10 (right to be accompanied at grievance hearings)
§46 ACAS Code (interaction of grievance and disciplinary processes)
https://www.acas.org.uk/acas-code-of-practice-on-disciplinary-and-grievance-procedures/html

sections.title: Hold a meeting, decide, and allow appeal
sections.summary: Following the meeting, decide on what action, if any, to take. The decision should be communicated to the employee in writing without unreasonable delay and, where appropriate, should set out what action the employer intends to take to resolve the grievance. The employee should be informed of the right to appeal if they are not satisfied with the action taken. The appeal should be dealt with impartially and, where possible, by a manager who has not previously been involved.
sections.text:
36. Following the meeting decide on what action, if any, to take. Decisions should be communicated to the employee, in writing, without unreasonable delay and, where appropriate, should set out what action the employer intends to take to resolve the grievance. The employee should be informed that they can appeal if they are not satisfied with the action taken.

38. Where an employee feels that their grievance has not been satisfactorily resolved they should appeal. They should let the employer know the grounds for their appeal without unreasonable delay and in writing.

40. The appeal should be dealt with impartially and, wherever possible, by a manager who has not previously been involved in the case.

41. The employee should be informed in writing of the results of the appeal hearing as soon as possible.

leadingCases.name: WA Goold (Pearmak) Ltd v McConnell [1995] IRLR 516
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the implied term of trust and confidence imposes a duty on the employer to reasonably and promptly provide a means by which a grievance can be raised and considered. A failure to provide or operate a grievance procedure may amount to a fundamental breach of contract entitling the employee to resign and claim constructive dismissal.
leadingCases.significance: Established that failure to provide a grievance procedure can amount to a fundamental breach of contract

refs: ERA 1996 s.3 (written statement must include grievance procedure or reference to it)
ERA 1996 s.10 (right to be accompanied)
https://www.acas.org.uk/acas-code-of-practice-on-disciplinary-and-grievance-procedures/html

sections.title: Overlapping grievance and disciplinary cases
sections.summary: Where an employee raises a grievance during a disciplinary process the disciplinary process may be temporarily suspended in order to deal with the grievance. Where the grievance and disciplinary cases are related it may be appropriate to deal with both issues concurrently. This is a common tactical situation in employment disputes where employees raise grievances to delay or derail disciplinary proceedings.
sections.text:
46. Where an employee raises a grievance during a disciplinary process the disciplinary process may be temporarily suspended in order to deal with the grievance. Where the grievance and disciplinary cases are related it may be appropriate to deal with both issues concurrently.

47. The provisions of this Code do not apply to grievances raised on behalf of two or more employees by a representative of a recognised trade union or other appropriate workplace representative. These are collective grievances and are normally addressed through collective agreements.

leadingCases.name: Samuel Smith Old Brewery (Tadcaster) v Marshall [2011] UKEAT/0488/10
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that an employer is not obliged to halt disciplinary proceedings simply because the employee has raised a grievance. The employer must exercise judgment about whether to deal with the grievance separately, concurrently, or to continue with the disciplinary process. What matters is overall fairness.
leadingCases.significance: Clarified that raising a grievance does not automatically suspend disciplinary proceedings

refs: §5–8 ACAS Code (investigation stage of disciplinary process)
https://www.acas.org.uk/acas-code-of-practice-on-disciplinary-and-grievance-procedures/html

pendingAmendments: ERA 2025 — probationary period procedure expected to interact with Code 1 (not yet in force)
Monitoring for potential Code revision following ERA 2025 commencement

parliamentaryOrigin.issuedUnder: Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992 s.199
parliamentaryOrigin.statutoryBasis: Statutory Code of Practice — approved by resolution of both Houses of Parliament
parliamentaryOrigin.legalEffect: Employment tribunals must take the Code into account (TULRCA 1992 s.207). Awards may be adjusted by up to 25% for unreasonable failure to comply (s.207A). The Code replaced the statutory dispute resolution procedures under the Employment Act 2002 (repealed by EA 2008 s.1).
parliamentaryOrigin.note: Code of Practice 1 is the most frequently cited ACAS Code in employment tribunal proceedings. It underpins every unfair dismissal case involving disciplinary or grievance procedures. The Code was fundamentally reformed by the Employment Act 2008 which abolished the prescriptive 3-step statutory procedures and replaced them with the Code-based approach.

precedingDocuments.title: Employment Act 2008 s.1-3
precedingDocuments.significance: Repealed the statutory dispute resolution procedures (Employment Act 2002 s.29-33 and Sch.2) and introduced the s.207A 25% adjustment mechanism. The current Code approach replaced the prescriptive 3-step procedures.

precedingDocuments.title: Gibbons Review: Better Dispute Resolution
precedingDocuments.significance: Michael Gibbons reviewed the statutory dispute resolution procedures and recommended their replacement with a simpler, more flexible Code-based approach. His recommendations were implemented by the Employment Act 2008.

leadingCases.name: Polkey v AE Dayton Services Ltd [1988] AC 344
leadingCases.heldText: A dismissal may be unfair solely because the employer failed to follow a fair procedure, even if the dismissal would have occurred in any event. Procedural fairness is an independent requirement.
leadingCases.significance: Foundation authority — procedural fairness is essential to fair dismissal.

leadingCases.name: BHS v Burchell [1980] ICR 303
leadingCases.heldText: For misconduct dismissals: the employer must genuinely believe in the misconduct, have reasonable grounds for that belief, and have carried out a reasonable investigation.
leadingCases.significance: The Burchell test — the three-stage test for misconduct dismissals.

leadingCases.name: Iceland Frozen Foods Ltd v Jones [1983] ICR 17
leadingCases.heldText: The question is whether the employer's decision fell within the range of reasonable responses, not whether the tribunal would have reached the same decision.
leadingCases.significance: The range of reasonable responses test for unfair dismissal.

leadingCases.name: Slade v Biggs [2022] UKEAT/0149/21
leadingCases.heldText: Wholesale failure to follow the ACAS Code warrants maximum 25% uplift under s.207A.
leadingCases.significance: Maximum s.207A uplift for wholesale Code failure.
